original_hypothesis.title: Perpetual Yield Delay via Micro-Distributions in Rebase Distributor
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `ERC20RebaseDistributor.distribute()` function resets the 30-day interpolation period (`DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD`) for ALL pending unminted rewards every time it is called. An attacker can repeatedly call `distribute(1 wei)` every few hours. Each call takes the current unminted rewards and stretches them over a new 30-day period, perpetually flattening the distribution curve and preventing rebasing CREDIT holders from ever receiving their full accumulated yield.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This mechanism allows a low-capital attacker to indefinitely lock the yield of all lenders, breaking the core economic incentive of the protocol. Validation requires modeling the interpolation curve under frequent micro-distributions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: targetTimestamp is unconditionally set to block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD on every distribute call

preconditions: Protocol has generated profit and distributed it to the rebase distributor
Attacker holds a tiny amount of CREDIT to use for distribution

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Frequent calls to distribute(1) constantly push the targetTimestamp 30 days into the future
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack; attacker spends 1 wei per call to deny thousands of CREDIT in yield to lenders.

economic_questions: What is the mathematical limit of yield extraction under continuous micro-distributions?
How much gas does the attacker need to spend to effectively lock the yield?
validation_requirements: Simulate the interpolation math over 60 days with daily 1 wei distributions.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Lenders receive near-zero yield, causing capital flight from the protocol

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test the exact interpolation output when targetTimestamp is constantly moved

validation_result.impact_summary: Each call to distribute() resets the 30-day interpolation period for ALL pending unminted rebase rewards, allowing an attacker to perpetually delay yield distribution to rebasing CREDIT holders by repeatedly calling distribute(1 wei).
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The distribute() function in ERC20RebaseDistributor.sol unconditionally resets both the __rebasingSharePrice and __unmintedRebaseRewards interpolation targets to block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD on every call. When a legitimate distribution of, say, 1000 CREDIT occurs, the share price interpolates linearly from its current value to a target over 30 days. If an attacker calls distribute(1) after 15 days, the current interpolated value (only halfway to the old target) becomes the new lastValue, and the remaining delta is stretched over a fresh 30-day period. The targetValue is correctly incremented by the new 1-wei amount, but the targetTimestamp reset means the remaining ~50% of the original 1000 CREDIT distribution is now spread over 30 more days instead of the remaining 15.

Mathematically, if an attacker calls distribute(1) every Δt seconds, each call releases approximately Δt/DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD of the remaining undistributed rewards and then stretches the rest over a full new 30-day period. With hourly calls (Δt=3600s), after 30 days only ~63% of rewards would have been delivered vs 100% normally. The attack costs 1 wei of CREDIT per call plus gas, making it extremely cheap compared to the yield it delays.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The distribute() function at ERC20RebaseDistributor.sol lines ~267-300 clearly shows the unconditional targetTimestamp reset. The lastValue is set to the current interpolated value and targetTimestamp is set to block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD regardless of how much pending interpolation remains. This stretches all previously pending rewards over a new 30-day window. The function is external with no access control, only requiring msg.sender to have `amount` tokens to burn.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The targetTimestamp is always set to block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD (30 days), regardless of how much of the existing interpolation has been consumed. The lastValue is set to the current interpolated value. This means all remaining undistributed rewards from previous distribute() calls are stretched over a new 30-day period. An attacker calling distribute(1) frequently re-stretches the pending rewards indefinitely.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker acquires minimal CREDIT (1 wei+) via PSM or other means
4. rebasingSharePrice() returns current interpolated price (partially through previous distribution)
5. endTimestamp = block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD resets target to 30 days out
6. newTargetSharePrice adds 1 wei contribution on top of OLD targetValue
9. Remaining undistributed rewards from prior distributions are now stretched over 30 more days
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is in distribute() at line where endTimestamp is set: `uint256 endTimestamp = block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD;`. This is then used for both __rebasingSharePrice.targetTimestamp and __unmintedRebaseRewards.targetTimestamp. The lastValue for both is set to the current interpolated value (not the target), so the remaining delta between current and target is now spread over a full new 30-day window. There is no access control on distribute() beyond requiring token ownership for the burn.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that are relevant to this vulnerability. The DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD is a constant (30 days) in the contract.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and realistic: 1) Attacker obtains a small amount of CREDIT (even 1e18 would provide enough for millions of calls). 2) Attacker sets up a bot to call distribute(1) every hour or more frequently. 3) Each call costs ~1 wei of CREDIT + gas (~50k gas per call). On L2s this is negligible. 4) The effect: legitimate profit distributions that normally interpolate over 30 days are perpetually stretched. With hourly calls, after 30 days only ~63% of a single distribution would have materialized, instead of 100%. With continuous calls, the yield curve is permanently flattened. The attacker's cost per day is ~24 calls × gas cost, which on Ethereum L1 would be roughly 0.001-0.01 ETH/day. The value at risk is the time-value of all rebasing CREDIT yield.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is a significant delay (not complete prevention) of yield distribution to all rebasing CREDIT holders. With sustained hourly micro-distributions, the effective delivery of rewards follows an exponential decay curve instead of linear interpolation, delivering only ~63% of expected yield after 30 days instead of 100%. This is a griefing attack that degrades protocol economics. While the share price still monotonically increases (INV-ECON-005 is technically not violated), the rate of increase is severely diminished. The hypothesis slightly overstates the impact as 'near 100% reduction' - the actual reduction depends on attack frequency but is approximately 37% at the 30-day mark with hourly calls.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a minimum distribution amount check (e.g., require amount >= some meaningful threshold) to make griefing expensive
Restrict distribute() to authorized callers only (e.g., ProfitManager)
Redesign the interpolation to accumulate new distributions additively without resetting the period for already-pending rewards (e.g., use a queue of interpolation segments)
Use a weighted combination where the remaining interpolation period for existing rewards is preserved and only the new amount uses a fresh 30-day period
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy CreditToken with rebase configuration. 2. Have address A enter rebase with 1000 CREDIT. 3. Call distribute(100) from another address. 4. Warp 15 days. Record balanceOf(A) - should show ~50% of rewards. 5. Call distribute(1) from attacker. 6. Warp 15 more days (total 30 days from original distribute). Record balanceOf(A) - should show significantly less than 100% of original rewards. 7. Compare with baseline (no griefing call) where balanceOf(A) shows full rewards after 30 days. 8. Repeat with multiple griefing calls at shorter intervals to measure the degradation curve.
uncertainties: The exact economic impact depends on attack frequency and the ratio of legitimate distributions to griefing calls
On L1 Ethereum, gas costs may make very frequent calls (every block) uneconomical, but on L2s this is very cheap
If there are frequent legitimate distributions happening anyway, the incremental damage from griefing calls may be lower
additional_testing_needed: Simulate 60 days of operation with legitimate daily distributions interleaved with hourly griefing calls to quantify actual yield reduction
Test edge cases where totalRebasingShares is very small or very large
Measure the gas cost of the attack on target deployment chains (L1 vs L2)
